before: Recorded in 1989, The Dual Role of Bob Brookmeyer showcases Bob Brookmeyer at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Cool Jazz. The Dual Role of Bob Brookmeyer remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Bob Brookmeyer's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: First released in 1955, The Dual Role of Bob Brookmeyer is a cool-jazz session led by Bob Brookmeyer. It appeared on Prestige (P-7066) and was later reissued by Original Jazz Classics. Tracks include "Rocky Scotch," "Under the Lilacs," and "Revelation."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate 1955, labels Prestige/Original Jazz Classics, tracklist.

before: Recorded in 1997, The Freedom Book showcases Booker Ervin at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Hard Bop. The Freedom Book remains a landmark of Hard Bop, cementing Booker Ervin's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: Released in 1963, The Freedom Book is a hard-bop date led by saxophonist Booker Ervin. It came out on Prestige (PR 7295). Tracks include "A Lunar Tune," "Cry Me Not," and "Grant's Stand."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: date 1963, releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate 1963, label Prestige PR 7295, tracklist.

before: Recorded in 2006, The Gigolo showcases Lee Morgan at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Bebop. The Gigolo remains a landmark of Bebop, cementing Lee Morgan's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: The Gigolo is an album by trumpeter Lee Morgan, recorded on June 25 and July 1, 1965 and released on Blue Note in 1968. Morgan leads a quintet with Wayne Shorter, Harold Mabern, Bob Cranshaw, and Billy Higgins. Tracks include the title cut, "Speedball," and "Trapped."
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): recorded June 25 & July 1 1965, released Blue Note 1968, quintet personnel; MusicBrainz tracklist.

before: Recorded in 2006, The Hawk Flies High showcases Coleman Hawkins at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Swing. The Hawk Flies High remains a landmark of Swing, cementing Coleman Hawkins's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: First released in 1957, The Hawk Flies High is a session led by saxophonist Coleman Hawkins, later reissued by Mobile Fidelity Sound Lab. Tracks include "Chant," "Juicy Fruit," and "Sancticity."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate 1957, reissue label Mobile Fidelity Sound Lab, tracklist.

before: The Lore (2025) captures Pat Metheny exploring jazz fusion territory on the FP label. Highlights of the session include "Bright Size Life", "Are You Going With Me?", and "Goin’ Ahead". As of 2024, he had been nominated for 4 Grammy Awards, including Best Jazz Instrumental Album for Guided Tour in 2014 and Still Dreaming in 2019.
after: The Lore (2025) is a Pat Metheny compilation released on the FP label. Highlights include "Bright Size Life," "Are You Going With Me?," and "Goin’ Ahead."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release (entityMatch yes): title, artist Pat Metheny, date 2025-03-27, label FP, secondaryType Compilation, tracklist. Dropped the Scott Colley Grammy sentence (wrong-entity wiki).

before: With The Most Happy Piano (1957), pianist Erroll Garner delivers a cool jazz program for the Columbia label. Among the tracks are "Girl of My Dreams" and "But Not for Me". It was first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra, and played a prominent part in the 1971 motion picture Play Misty for Me.
after: With The Most Happy Piano (1957), pianist Erroll Garner delivers a cool jazz program for the Columbia label. Among the tracks are "Girl of My Dreams" and "But Not for Me".
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, artist Erroll Garner, date 1957, label Columbia CL 939, tracklist. Dropped the final sentence (Garner-bio graft about the song 'Misty', wrong-entity wiki).

before: Recorded in 1987, The Sermon! showcases Jimmy Smith at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Hard Bop. The Sermon! remains a landmark of Hard Bop, cementing Jimmy Smith's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: The Sermon! is an album by organist Jimmy Smith, recorded on August 25, 1957 and February 25, 1958 and released on Blue Note in 1959. The three-track set comprises "The Sermon," "J.O.S.," and "Flamingo."
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): recorded Aug 25 1957 & Feb 25 1958, released Blue Note 1959; MusicBrainz tracklist.

before: A 1994 Squirrel Thing Recordings date, Tidal Wave finds Fletcher Henderson in an early jazz setting. The date includes "Hocus Pocus" and "Hocus Pocus (alternative version)".
after: A 1994 Squirrel Thing Recordings compilation, Tidal Wave gathers early-jazz sides by Fletcher Henderson. It includes "Hocus Pocus," "Hocus Pocus (alternative version)," and the title track.
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, artist Fletcher Henderson, date 1994, label Squirrel Thing Recordings, tracklist (Hocus Pocus, Hocus Pocus alt, Tidal Wave). Prose uses only MB-confirmed tracks.

before: Recorded in 2013, Undercurrent showcases Bill Evans at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Cool Jazz. Undercurrent remains a landmark of Cool Jazz, cementing Bill Evans's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: Undercurrent is a 1962 album by pianist Bill Evans and guitarist Jim Hall, recorded in the spring of 1962. The duo opens with an up-tempo reading of "My Funny Valentine" and includes Hall's "Romain" and John Lewis's "Skating in Central Park."
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): 1962 album by Bill Evans and Jim Hall, recorded spring 1962, 'My Funny Valentine' as the up-tempo opener, Hall's 'Romain', Lewis's 'Skating in Central Park'; MusicBrainz tracklist.

before: Recorded in 1988, Way Out West showcases Sonny Rollins at the height of their creative powers, offering one of the finest expressions of Bebop. Way Out West remains a landmark of Bebop, cementing Sonny Rollins's reputation as one of the defining voices of the era and earning consistent praise from critics worldwide.
after: Way Out West is a 1957 album by saxophonist Sonny Rollins, with bassist Ray Brown and drummer Shelly Manne. It was his first record made with only bass and drums, no chordal instrument. Tracks include "I'm an Old Cowhand," "Come, Gone," and the title cut; the 1988 CD reissue added alternate takes.
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): 1957 album, Ray Brown and Shelly Manne, first bass-and-drums-only date, 1988 CD reissue with alternate takes; MusicBrainz tracklist.

before: Alice Coltrane's most orchestrally ambitious album, featuring her harp and organ work drenched in lush string arrangements. The music travels through cosmic landscapes, from the devotional opening of 'My Favorite Things' to the transcendent closer 'A Galaxy of Love.'. Represented Coltrane's boldest fusion of jazz, Indian classical music, and orchestral grandeur. The album's cosmic spirituality has been rediscovered and sampled extensively in hip-hop and electronic music.
after: World Galaxy is a 1972 Alice Coltrane album on the Impulse! label. The five-track program opens with "My Favorite Things" and closes with "A Love Supreme," alongside "Galaxy in Satchidananda" and "Galaxy in Turiya."
evidenceUsed: MusicBrainz release: title, artist Alice Coltrane, date 1972, label Impulse! AS-9218, tracklist (opener 'My Favorite Things', closer 'A Love Supreme'). Removed invented closer 'A Galaxy of Love' and unverifiable personnel/sampling claims.

before: Abdullah Ibrahim (born Dollar Brand) is the most important South African jazz musician. His hypnotic 'Mannenberg' became the anthem of the anti-apartheid movement. His music blends Cape Town's musical traditions with American jazz and African spirituality.
after: Abdullah Ibrahim (born Adolph Johannes Brand, formerly known as Dollar Brand) is the most important South African jazz musician. His hypnotic 'Mannenberg' became the anthem of the anti-apartheid movement. His music blends Cape Town's musical traditions with American jazz and African spirituality.
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): 'born Adolph Johannes Brand on 9 October 1934, previously known as Dollar Brand'. Fixed only the birth-name claim; kept the rest verbatim.

before: Andy Bey is a vocalist and pianist possessing one of the deepest, most resonant baritone voices in jazz. Despite decades of underappreciation, his late-career recordings for Savant and HighNote revealed a master interpreter of standards and spiritual jazz. His self-accompanied style draws on the full history of African-American vocal music, from spirituals to bebop, delivered with a gravitas that few singers can match.
after: Andy Bey (1939–2025) was a vocalist and pianist who possessed one of the deepest, most resonant baritone voices in jazz. Despite decades of underappreciation, his late-career recordings for HighNote revealed a master interpreter of standards and spiritual jazz. His self-accompanied style draws on the full history of African-American vocal music, from spirituals to bebop, delivered with a gravitas that few singers can match.
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): born Oct 28 1939, died April 26 2025; HighNote albums (2013, 2014). Shifted to past tense with life dates; dropped 'Savant' (no Savant title in the evidence discography).

before: Baby Face Willette was a mysterious Hammond B-3 organist who recorded only two albums for Blue Note before vanishing from the jazz scene entirely. 'Face to Face' and 'Stop and Listen' are raw, soulful recordings that capture the gritty side of early 1960s soul jazz. Little is known about his life before or after these sessions, making him one of jazz's most enigmatic figures.
after: Baby Face Willette (1933–1971) was a Hammond organist who recorded two leader albums for Blue Note, 'Face to Face' and 'Stop and Listen' (both 1961), raw and soulful recordings that capture the gritty side of early 1960s soul jazz. He came up through gospel and toured as a pianist before switching to organ around 1958. After the Blue Note dates he returned to Chicago, formed a trio in 1963, and recorded two further albums for Argo.
evidenceUsed: Wikipedia (entityMatch yes): 1933–1971, Hammond organ, gospel roots, switched to organ c.1958, two Blue Note leader albums (1961), formed Chicago trio 1963 and recorded two more for Argo. Removed 'vanished entirely' and 'little is known'.
